name: C++ Lifetime Profile (P1179)
summary: Local-only static analysis (Herb Sutter, Neil MacIntosh) that classifies types into generalized Owner/Pointer categories and detects dangling/use-after-free at compile time without whole-program annotation. Partially shipped in MSVC since 2019 and upstreamed into Clang trunk as `-Wlifetime-safety` (Clang 23+).

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
P1179 is a local-only static analysis that identifies generalized "Owner"/"Pointer" types via type categories to detect dangling/use-after-free at compile time without whole-program annotation. It uses acyclic CFG analysis with ~5% compile-time overhead; it identifies Owner (e.g. `unique_ptr`, `string`) versus Pointer (e.g. `string_view`, iterators) generically; and uses `gsl::Owner`, `gsl::Pointer`, and `clang::lifetimebound` attributes to refine the inference where the heuristic alone is insufficient.

The distinguishing design choice is that no whole-program or viral annotation is required: the analysis is intra-procedural, recognizes vocabulary types from the standard library by category, and emits warnings rather than rejecting programs. This is the closest existing C++ has to a lifetime-aware diagnostic without committing to a borrow-checker's expressivity or annotation discipline.

sections.title: MSVC and Clang implementations
sections.content:
Partially shipped in MSVC (warnings 26486–26489 in C++ Core Check) since 2019. Upstreamed to Clang trunk as `-Wlifetime-safety` (Clang 23+). The MSVC implementation is part of `/analyze` and integrates with the existing C++ Core Check warnings.

Status (as of 2026-04): lifetime/profile work is not normative C++26. The active path is a non-normative language-safety white paper plus compiler experiments and flags, with possible later standardization into a lifetime profile. The vendor implementations remain the practical access point — programmers turn the warnings on, fix or annotate the flagged code, and proceed without language-level changes.
